metadata.title: Why Two Checkpoints Fail When One Virus Infects
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = intact / functional / normal cell circuit; CRIMSON = disabled / lost / virus-hijacked
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no HPV type taxonomy beyond naming 16 and 18; no CIN grading system detail; no other viral carcinogens (EBV, KSHV); no HPV vaccine mechanism; no integration vs episomal HPV distinction. All illustrative numbers labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.title: Title card
beats.narration_text: HPV-16 achieves in one infection what normally requires two independent somatic mutations accumulating over years: it disables both p53 and Rb simultaneously.

viz.headline: Why Two Checkpoints Fail When One Virus Infects

build.suggested: suggested prompt: HPV-16 achieves in one infection what normally requires two independent somatic mutations accumulating over years: it disables both p53 and Rb simultaneously.

beats.title: Cervical cell under HPV assault

props.headline: HPV-16 infects a cervical epithelial cell. Within hours, two viral proteins…

beats.narration_text: HPV-16 infects a cervical epithelial cell. Within hours, two viral proteins begin degrading the cell's tumor suppressors — without making a single detectable DNA mutation.

viz.description: Microscopy-style cross-section of a cervical epithelial cell being contacted by small virus particles. Cool clinical newsprint treatment.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: HPV-16 infects a cervical epithelial cell. Within hours, two viral proteins begin degrading the cell's tumor suppressors — without making a single detectable DN

beats.title: The question — one infection, two losses
beats.narration_text: Losing both p53 and Rb normally requires two independent somatic mutations — a rare coincidence. HPV achieves both losses from a single infection event. The question: how does one virus do the work of two mutations?

viz.question: Why does one infection do the work of two mutations?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Losing both p53 and Rb normally requires two independent somatic mutations — a rare coincidence. HPV achieves both losses from a single infection event. The que

beats.title: The two-hit expectation — normal mutation logic
beats.narration_text: Standard tumor suppressor loss: one somatic mutation disables one allele, a second independent mutation hits the other. Two events, both rare, both required. The probability of both hitting the same cell in the same generation is low.

viz.description: Two independent mutation arrows, each striking one gene, shown sequentially over a timeline. CRIMSON arrows. TEAL = intact gene, CRIMSON = mutated.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Standard tumor suppressor loss: one somatic mutation disables one allele, a second independent mutation hits the other. Two events, both rare, both required. Th

beats.title: p53 — the damage guardian
beats.narration_text: p53 is the DNA-damage checkpoint. Damage accumulates, p53 levels rise, the cell cycle halts, repair begins. Without p53: no halt, no repair. Damaged DNA passes directly into the next division.

viz.description: Single-arm circuit: DNA damage signal -> p53 node -> halt signal. All TEAL, functional.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: p53 is the DNA-damage checkpoint. Damage accumulates, p53 levels rise, the cell cycle halts, repair begins. Without p53: no halt, no repair. Damaged DNA passes

beats.title: Rb — the gate on DNA replication
beats.narration_text: Rb holds E2F — a pro-replication transcription factor — sequestered. A controlled cyclin signal releases E2F and opens S phase. Rb is the gate. Without it, E2F is constitutively free and the cell replicates regardless of conditions.

viz.description: Rb box holding E2F. Cyclin signal -> Rb releases E2F -> S-phase genes on. All TEAL, functional.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Rb holds E2F — a pro-replication transcription factor — sequestered. A controlled cyclin signal releases E2F and opens S phase. Rb is the gate. Without it, E2F

beats.title: Section card — how HPV does it
beats.narration_text: Two viral proteins. Two targets. One infection event.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two viral proteins. Two targets. One infection event.

beats.title: E6 hijacks ubiquitin machinery to destroy p53
beats.narration_text: E6, the first viral protein, recruits E6AP — the cell's own ubiquitin ligase — and redirects it to tag p53 for proteasomal degradation. The TP53 gene is intact. The p53 protein is gone. The cell's own cleanup machinery becomes the weapon.

viz.description: E6 (CRIMSON) grabs E6AP ubiquitin machinery, arrow to p53 node, p53 shrinks away and disappears.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: E6, the first viral protein, recruits E6AP — the cell's own ubiquitin ligase — and redirects it to tag p53 for proteasomal degradation. The TP53 gene is intact.

beats.title: E7 binds Rb and forces E2F free
beats.narration_text: E7, the second viral protein, binds Rb directly and forces the Rb-E2F complex apart. E2F is released — not by a cyclin signal, but by a viral crowbar. S-phase genes activate regardless of whether the cell received a growth signal.

viz.description: E7 (CRIMSON) wedges between Rb and E2F, Rb shown grayed/disabled, E2F escaping upward toward S-phase genes.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: E7, the second viral protein, binds Rb directly and forces the Rb-E2F complex apart. E2F is released — not by a cyclin signal, but by a viral crowbar. S-phase g

beats.title: Both circuits dark — the split
beats.narration_text: Both circuits disabled simultaneously. One infection event. No mutation in TP53, no mutation in RB1 — both proteins absent. The DNA-damage gate is dark. The S-phase gate is permanently open.

viz.description: Two parallel arms shown side by side. Upper arm: E6 -> p53 destroyed (CRIMSON). Lower arm: E7 -> Rb disabled, E2F free (CRIMSON). Both converge on a single node: checkpoint-free cell.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Both circuits disabled simultaneously. One infection event. No mutation in TP53, no mutation in RB1 — both proteins absent. The DNA-damage gate is dark. The S-p

beats.title: Not mutation — protein-level hijacking
beats.narration_text: Critical limit for sequencing-based approaches: E6 and E7 alter no DNA letters. TP53 and RB1 read intact on a sequencing panel. The proteins are gone. A sequence-only oncology workup classifies this cell as normal — and misses the mechanism entirely.

viz.description: Two columns: left = DNA sequence (TEAL, intact TP53/RB1 shown as clean bands), right = protein level (CRIMSON crossed-out p53, crossed-out Rb). Contrast the two layers.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Critical limit for sequencing-based approaches: E6 and E7 alter no DNA letters. TP53 and RB1 read intact on a sequencing panel. The proteins are gone. A sequenc

beats.title: Permissive conditions for further mutation
beats.narration_text: With no p53 to arrest and no Rb to slow replication, the cell is now permissive for further damage. Each division risks an additional mutation. Over years, that accumulation drives progression toward invasion.

viz.description: A simple damage-accumulation timeline: small marks accumulate on a bar representing the cell's genome over time, unchecked, each division adding more.

beats.title: Example — UV damage in a normal vs HPV-infected cell
beats.narration_text: UV damage hits two cervical cells. Normal cell: p53 rises within minutes, cycle halts, repair begins. HPV-infected cell — same UV, same damage — no p53 response, no halt. E6 cleared p53. E7 freed E2F. The damage enters S phase unrestricted. Illustrative.

viz.description: Left cell (TEAL): UV arrow -> p53 accumulates (TEAL bar rising) -> HALT. Right cell (CRIMSON): UV arrow -> no p53 (flat, crossed) -> S phase proceeds, damage carried forward.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: UV damage hits two cervical cells. Normal cell: p53 rises within minutes, cycle halts, repair begins. HPV-infected cell — same UV, same damage — no p53 response

beats.title: Endcard
beats.narration_text: One infection, two tumor suppressors neutralized — not by mutation, but by viral proteins that commandeer the cell's own degradation and displacement machinery. That is why HPV compresses years of somatic evolution into a single exposure event.

viz.question: Why does one infection do the work of two mutations?
viz.answer: E6 and E7 hijack the cell's own machinery to eliminate p53 and Rb without touching a single DNA letter.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: One infection, two tumor suppressors neutralized — not by mutation, but by viral proteins that commandeer the cell's own degradation and displacement machinery.
